(4) Shortest AS path — Path 1=3 hops; Path 3=2 hops; Path 3 wins. Result: Path 3 selected. Key lesson: LOCAL_PREF is evaluated BEFORE AS path length — Path 2's shorter path is irrelevant once it loses on LOCAL_PREF. LOCAL_PREF is an iBGP attribute used to influence egress selection within an AS. Default LOCAL_PREF = 100 if not set.
aspect_key: bgp path selection with local_pref and as path tiebreak

question: A technician opens PuTTY to connect to a Cisco router via console cable on COM4 but sees only garbled characters. List the four serial settings that must match Cisco defaults and their required values.
choice_a: Speed 115200; data bits 7; parity even; stop bits 2
choice_b: Speed (baud rate) 9600; data bits 8; parity none; stop bits 1 — this 9600/8/N/1 combination is the Cisco default for console access; any single mismatch causes garbled output
choice_c: Speed 9600; data bits 8; parity odd; stop bits 1 — parity odd is required for Cisco console authentication
choice_d: Speed 19200; data bits 8; parity none; stop bits 1 — 19200 is required after IOS 15.x
explanation: Cisco console defaults: Speed=9600 bps; Data bits=8; Parity=None; Stop bits=1. Written as 9600-8-N-1. PuTTY path: Connection → Serial → Serial line=COM4; Speed=9600. Session type=Serial. Garbled output troubleshooting: wrong baud rate is most common — try 115200 for newer ASA/ISR devices. Wrong parity (e.g. even vs none) produces garbage. Flow control should be set to None. Hardware: RJ45-to-DB9 rollover (light blue) cable or USB console cable. If speed is wrong each character arrives as wrong value → unreadable output. Confirm COM port number in Device Manager.
aspect_key: cisco console serial settings 9600-8-n-1

question: A host at 172.20.15.200/20 tries to ping 172.20.16.1. The ping fails. Using bitwise AND determine both hosts' network addresses and explain why a router is required.
choice_a: 172.20.15.200/20 and 172.20.16.1 are on the same /20 network — the ping fails due to a firewall rule
choice_c: For 172.20.15.200 the network is 172.20.15.0; for 172.20.16.1 it is 172.20.16.0 — these differ only in the third octet so they are in the same /20
choice_d: The ping fails because 172.20.16.1 is the broadcast address of 172.20.15.200/20
explanation: /20 mask = 255.255.240.0. Third octet calculation: 240=11110000. 15=00001111 → AND=00000000=0. 16=00010000 → AND=00010000=16. Network 1: 172.20.0.0/20 (range 172.20.0.0–172.20.15.255). Network 2: 172.20.16.0/20 (range 172.20.16.0–172.20.31.255). /20 boundary falls exactly between .15.x and .16.x because 256-240=16 (third octet increments by 16). Router required to cross the /20 boundary. Trap: students see same first two octets and assume same network — the /20 mask uses 4 bits of the third octet for network identification.
aspect_key: bitwise AND across a /20 subnet boundary

question: An organization receives the public block 203.0.113.0/24 from its ISP. The ISP obtained that block from ARIN. Trace the complete allocation chain starting from the authority that originally managed that address space and explain each step.
choice_a: The allocation chain is: ICANN → ISP → organization — ARIN is not involved in public IP allocation
choice_b: The complete chain is: IANA (global authority; managed by ICANN) allocates large address blocks to ARIN (the RIR for North America); ARIN assigns the 203.0.113.0/24 block (or a larger parent block) to the ISP after reviewing the ISP's justified need; the ISP sub-allocates 203.0.113.0/24 to the organization; the organization assigns individual addresses to its hosts
choice_c: IANA assigns addresses directly to organizations — RIRs only handle IPv6 allocation; not IPv4
choice_d: The chain is: DNS root servers → IANA → ISP → organization — DNS root servers control IP address allocation for top-level domains
explanation: Full allocation chain: IANA (Internet Assigned Numbers Authority; operated by ICANN) → global IP address space coordinator. Allocates large blocks (e.g. /8s) to 5 RIRs. ARIN (American Registry for Internet Numbers) → RIR for North America. Receives blocks from IANA; assigns to ISPs and large orgs based on demonstrated need. ISP → receives block from ARIN (e.g. 203.0.113.0/24 or larger). Sub-allocates to customers. Organization → assigns individual IPs to hosts; servers; network devices. Note: 203.0.113.0/24 is actually an RFC5737 documentation range — in real life this would be a legitimately allocated public block. IANA exhausted IPv4 in 2011; RIRs now manage remaining space.
aspect_key: full iana rir isp organization chain

question: What DNS configuration allows internal clients to resolve both internal hostnames and external internet hostnames when the internal DNS server has no direct internet connectivity?
choice_b: Configure a DNS forwarder on the internal DNS server pointing to an internet-capable DNS resolver (such as 8.8.8.8 or the ISP's DNS server) — the internal server answers queries for corp.local from its own zone data; for all other queries it forwards to the external resolver which has internet access
choice_c: Add every external hostname as a static A record in the internal DNS zone — this eliminates the need for forwarding
choice_d: Disable DNS on the internal server and point all clients directly to 8.8.8.8 so internal names resolve via the internet
correct_choice: Configure the internal DNS server as the authoritative server for the entire internet DNS namespace
explanation: B
concept_key: DNS forwarder configuration: internal DNS server is authoritative for corp.local zone (answers fileserver.corp.local queries from local zone data). For unknown names (e.g. www.example.com): server forwards query to configured forwarder (8.8.8.8). Forwarder resolves on behalf of internal server; returns answer. Internal server caches and returns to client. Result: internal names resolve locally; external names resolve via forwarder. Conditional forwarder variant: forward specific external domains to specific resolvers. Client perspective: all queries go to internal DNS — no direct internet DNS exposure. Benefit: central control; caching; logging of all DNS queries.
aspect_key: dns administration context
source_path: dns forwarder for split internal external resolution

question: A classful Class B network 172.16.0.0 has been assigned to an organization. Without subnetting; how many host addresses are available; what is the default mask; and how does this compare to the actual RFC1918 172.16.0.0/12 block?
choice_a: Class B 172.16.0.0 with default /16 mask provides 2^16-2=65534 usable hosts; the RFC1918 172.16.0.0/12 block is larger — it spans from 172.16.0.0 to 172.31.255.255 providing 2^20=1048576 total addresses
choice_b: Class B 172.16.0.0 with default /16 mask provides 2^8-2=254 hosts — the same as a Class C network
choice_c: Class B 172.16.0.0 default mask is /24 providing 2^8-2=254 hosts per subnet
choice_d: Class B networks have a default mask of /12 — so 172.16.0.0 classful and RFC1918 are identical
explanation: Classful Class B: default mask = /16 (255.255.0.0). Host bits = 32-16 = 16. Total addresses: 2^16 = 65536. Usable hosts: 65536-2 = 65534 (subtract network and broadcast). Range: 172.16.0.0 to 172.16.255.255. RFC1918 172.16.0.0/12: /12 mask = 255.240.0.0. Host bits = 32-12 = 20. Total: 2^20 = 1048576. Range: 172.16.0.0 to 172.31.255.255. Comparison: /12 block is 16× larger than the classful /16. The /12 encompasses 172.16.x.x through 172.31.x.x — 16 class B networks. This distinction matters: RFC1918 assigns the entire /12; but individual organizations typically subnet within it.
aspect_key: class b default mask vs rfc1918 172.16/12 scope

question: An IPv6 address is written as FE80::1. What type of address is this; what is its scope; and why does this address appear on every IPv6-enabled interface even without any manual configuration or DHCPv6?
choice_a: FE80::1 is a global unicast address — it is manually assigned and only appears after explicit configuration
choice_b: FE80::1 is a link-local address — addresses beginning with FE80::/10 are automatically generated on every IPv6-enabled interface using the EUI-64 process or random generation; they are scoped to the local link only and are never routed beyond that segment; their purpose is neighbor discovery; router discovery; and stateless autoconfiguration
choice_c: FE80::1 is a multicast address used for router advertisement messages to all hosts on a segment
choice_d: FE80::1 is a loopback address equivalent to 127.0.0.1 in IPv4 — it tests the local IPv6 stack without sending traffic on the wire
explanation: IPv6 link-local addresses: prefix FE80::/10 (FE80 through FEBF). Scope: link-local (Layer 2 segment only; not routed). Autoconfiguration: every IPv6-capable interface generates a link-local address automatically — no DHCP or manual config needed. Generation methods: EUI-64 (derived from MAC address with FF:FE inserted); or random (RFC 4941 privacy extensions). Purpose: Neighbor Discovery Protocol (replaces ARP); Router Solicitation/Advertisement; Stateless Address Autoconfiguration (SLAAC). Notation: FE80::1 expands to FE80:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001. Always present: even if IPv6 is disabled at network level; link-local auto-assigns on the interface.
aspect_key: link-local fe80 prefix autoconfiguration

question: A company has two internet connections: ISP-A and ISP-B. Their edge router runs BGP with both ISPs. They want all outbound traffic to prefer ISP-A unless it is unavailable. Which BGP attribute is manipulated to achieve this; on which router is it configured; and what happens if ISP-A fails?
choice_a: BGP MED (Multi-Exit Discriminator) is set higher on ISP-A's path — MED is the primary outbound path attribute and higher values are preferred
choice_b: LOCAL_PREF is set higher on the routes learned from ISP-A (e.g. LOCAL_PREF 200) versus ISP-B (LOCAL_PREF 100) — LOCAL_PREF is configured on the organization's own edge router; it influences outbound path selection within the AS; if ISP-A's BGP session goes down those routes lose their LOCAL_PREF 200 and routes from ISP-B (LOCAL_PREF 100) become the best path automatically
choice_c: AS path prepending is applied to ISP-B advertisements to make the ISP-A path appear shorter — AS path prepending controls inbound traffic; not outbound
choice_d: LOCAL_PREF is configured on the ISP's router — the organization requests ISP-A to set a higher LOCAL_PREF for all routes
explanation: Outbound traffic engineering with BGP: LOCAL_PREF = iBGP attribute (shared within the AS). Set on the edge router when routes are received from ISPs. ISP-A routes: set LOCAL_PREF 200. ISP-B routes: set LOCAL_PREF 100. BGP selects highest LOCAL_PREF → all outbound traffic exits via ISP-A. Failover: if ISP-A BGP session drops → routes with LOCAL_PREF 200 withdraw → ISP-B routes (100) become best path → traffic automatically reroutes. Convergence time: typically seconds to minutes depending on BGP timers. Contrast: MED influences inbound traffic from a neighboring AS; AS path prepending influences inbound from multiple upstream ASes.
aspect_key: local_pref for outbound path preference and failover

question: A network engineer needs to determine if host 192.168.50.130 belongs to the subnet 192.168.50.128/25. Without a calculator; explain the binary test and state the result.
choice_a: 192.168.50.130 is NOT in 192.168.50.128/25 because the host portion (130-128=2) exceeds the /25 boundary
choice_b: To test: apply the /25 mask (255.255.255.128) to 192.168.50.130 using bitwise AND. Fourth octet: 130=10000010 AND 128=10000000 = 10000000 = 128. Result: 192.168.50.128 — which equals the network address 192.168.50.128; therefore 192.168.50.130 IS in the subnet 192.168.50.128/25
choice_c: 192.168.50.130 cannot be in a /25 subnet because .130 exceeds the /25 boundary of .128
choice_d: To test membership divide the host address by the subnet size: 130÷128=1.015 — a non-integer result means the host is not in the subnet
explanation: /25 network test: mask = 255.255.255.128 = 11111111.11111111.11111111.10000000. 130 = 10000010. 128 = 10000000. 130 AND 128 = 10000000 = 128. Network address = 192.168.50.128 ✓ (matches the subnet). Subnet range: 192.168.50.128 (network) to 192.168.50.255 (broadcast). 192.168.50.128/25 range: .128–.255 (128 addresses; 126 usable). 192.168.50.130 is within .128–.255 → confirmed member. Quick mental check: /25 splits a /24 in half at .128; any address .128–.254 is in the upper half; .130 qualifies.
aspect_key: subnet membership test for /25

question: An administrator configured a Cisco switch with a Telnet password and enable secret. Six months later they need remote access but have forgotten both passwords. The console cable is available. Describe the complete password recovery procedure and which files are involved.
choice_a: There is no password recovery for Cisco switches — the only option is to buy a replacement switch
choice_b: Boot the switch to ROMMON by holding the MODE button during power-on; run flash_init to mount flash; rename or delete config.text (the startup config containing the passwords); boot the switch — it starts with no passwords; reconnect via console; reconfigure and save; optionally restore previous config if it was renamed rather than deleted
choice_c: Boot to ROMMON and run the password-recovery command — this is built into ROMMON and resets passwords without deleting configuration
choice_d: Connect via Telnet using the default factory password "cisco" — all Cisco devices have this backdoor password for recovery purposes
explanation: Cisco switch password recovery steps: 1. Connect console cable. 2. Power off switch. 3. Hold MODE button and power on → release when ROMMON prompt appears. 4. flash_init → mounts flash filesystem. 5. dir flash: → locate config.text. 6. rename flash:config.text flash:config.old (safer than delete — preserves config). 7. boot → switch boots with no startup config (no passwords). 8. After boot: copy flash:config.old startup-config. 9. reload → switch loads old config. 10. In privileged mode: enable secret <new-password>. 11. copy running-config startup-config. Files involved: config.text (startup config with passwords). vlan.dat (not relevant to password recovery).
aspect_key: password recovery procedure using rommon

question: A Class A address 10.0.0.0 is used by a large enterprise. They need to create exactly 1000 subnets while maximizing host addresses per subnet. What prefix length should be used and how many usable hosts does each subnet provide?
choice_a: Use /22 — this gives 1022 subnets and 1022 hosts which is not balanced
choice_b: Starting from /8 (Class A default); need to borrow enough bits to create ≥ 1000 subnets. 2^10 = 1024 ≥ 1000 → borrow 10 bits → prefix /18. Each /18 subnet has 32-18=14 host bits → 2^14-2 = 16382 usable hosts per subnet
choice_c: Starting from /8; borrow 9 bits: 2^9=512 < 1000 (insufficient); borrow 10 bits: 2^10=1024 ≥ 1000 → /18 is correct; but borrowing 11 bits (/19) gives 2048 subnets with only 8190 hosts — /18 is the minimum that meets the 1000-subnet requirement
choice_d: Use /24 — this gives 65536 subnets of Class A with 254 hosts each
explanation: Subnet calculation from Class A /8: need 1000 subnets. 2^9 = 512 (insufficient). 2^10 = 1024 ≥ 1000 → borrow 10 bits. New prefix: /8 + 10 = /18. Subnet mask: 255.255.192.0. Host bits remaining: 32-18 = 14. Usable hosts per subnet: 2^14-2 = 16382. Total subnets: 1024. Summary: 10.0.0.0/18; 10.0.64.0/18; 10.0.128.0/18; etc. Tradeoff: borrowing minimum bits (10) maximizes remaining host bits. Borrowing 11 bits (/19) would give 2048 subnets but only 8190 hosts — acceptable if more subnets needed later.
aspect_key: class a subnetting to meet subnet count requirement

question: A technician uses nslookup to query an internal DNS server for an external hostname and receives the correct IP. They then query the same hostname directly against 8.8.8.8 and also receive the correct IP. However; users report the hostname resolves to the wrong IP when browsing. What is the most likely cause?
choice_a: The DNS server has a TTL of 0 on all records — this forces re-resolution each time and causes inconsistency
choice_b: The client machine has a stale DNS cache entry overriding both DNS servers — the client's local cache (populated before the DNS record changed) is returning the old IP; running ipconfig /flushdns on the client would clear the cached entry and allow fresh resolution
choice_c: The browser uses a different DNS protocol than nslookup — browsers use mDNS while nslookup uses standard DNS
choice_d: Both DNS servers are returning incorrect data — a BGP route hijack is redirecting DNS traffic
explanation: DNS resolution order on Windows: (1) Local hosts file (C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts). (2) Local DNS resolver cache (ipconfig /displaydns to view). (3) DNS server query. nslookup bypasses the local cache and queries the server directly — so nslookup showing correct results while browsing shows wrong results points to a stale local cache. Fix: ipconfig /flushdns → clears resolver cache. Verify: ipconfig /displaydns before and after flush. Also check hosts file for manual overrides. TTL: DNS caches entries for the record's TTL duration — a recently changed record may be cached with the old value for hours.
aspect_key: stale client dns cache vs server resolution

question: A netstat -an output shows a local address of 0.0.0.0:80 in LISTEN state and a local address of 127.0.0.1:3306 in LISTEN state. What is the security difference between these two bindings?
choice_a: Both bindings are equally accessible — the IP address in the local address field is only used for logging purposes
choice_b: 0.0.0.0:80 means the web service accepts connections on ALL network interfaces (accessible from any IP that can reach this host); 127.0.0.1:3306 means the database service is bound only to the loopback interface and is inaccessible from any remote host — this is a security best practice for services that should only be accessed locally by the web application on the same machine
choice_c: 0.0.0.0:80 means IPv6 is disabled; 127.0.0.1:3306 means IPv6 is enabled on the database
choice_d: 127.0.0.1:3306 is more exposed because loopback addresses are routed to all interfaces on modern operating systems
explanation: Socket binding security: 0.0.0.0 = wildcard bind — service listens on all interfaces (LAN; WAN; loopback). Any host that can reach the server on port 80 can connect. 127.0.0.1 = loopback bind — packets to 127.x.x.x are handled entirely in software; never reach the NIC. No remote host can connect to 127.0.0.1:3306 regardless of firewall rules. Best practice: databases (MySQL/MariaDB 3306; PostgreSQL 5432) bind to 127.0.0.1 — only local applications (web server on same host) can connect. Web servers bind to 0.0.0.0:80 or 0.0.0.0:443 to accept internet connections. Defense in depth: even if firewall is misconfigured; 127.0.0.1 binding prevents remote access.
aspect_key: netstat binding 0.0.0.0 vs 127.0.0.1 security

question: How many bits does an IPv6 address contain; how many hex digits are needed to represent it fully; and what is the maximum decimal value of a single 16-bit group?
choice_a: IPv6 contains 64 bits; needs 8 hex digits; and the maximum value per group is 255
choice_b: IPv6 contains 128 bits; needs 32 hex digits total (displayed as 8 groups of 4 hex digits); and the maximum decimal value of a single 16-bit group is 65535 (2^16-1 = 0xFFFF)
choice_c: IPv6 contains 128 bits; needs 16 hex digits (one per byte); and the maximum per group is 4095
choice_d: IPv6 contains 256 bits to provide the expanded address space; each group is 32 bits with maximum value 4294967295
explanation: IPv6 math: 128 bits total. Each hex digit = 4 bits. 128 ÷ 4 = 32 hex digits. Displayed in 8 groups of 4 hex digits (8 × 16 bits = 128). Maximum per group: 0xFFFF = 1111111111111111 binary = 2^16-1 = 65535 decimal. Example full address: 2001:0DB8:85A3:0000:0000:8A2E:0370:7334. Each group range: 0000 (0) to FFFF (65535). Comparison: IPv4 = 32 bits; 4 octets; max octet = 255 (2^8-1). IPv6 group = 16 bits vs IPv4 octet = 8 bits — IPv6 groups are twice as wide. Total IPv6 addresses: 2^128 ≈ 3.4×10^38.
aspect_key: ipv6 bit count hex digit count and group max value

question: An engineer is given a /16 block and asked to subnet it into the maximum number of /29 subnets. How many /29 subnets fit; and how many usable hosts does each provide?
choice_a: A /16 contains 256 /29 subnets with 6 usable hosts each
choice_b: A /16 contains 2^(29-16) = 2^13 = 8192 /29 subnets; each /29 has 32-29=3 host bits → 2^3-2 = 6 usable hosts per subnet
choice_c: A /16 can be divided into 4096 /29 subnets each with 8 usable hosts
choice_d: A /16 divided into /29 subnets gives 1024 subnets with 30 usable hosts each
explanation: /16 to /29 calculation: bits borrowed = 29-16 = 13. Number of /29 subnets: 2^13 = 8192. Each /29 host bits: 32-29 = 3. Total addresses per /29: 2^3 = 8. Usable: 8-2 = 6 hosts. Mask for /29: 255.255.255.248 (248=11111000). Example: 10.0.0.0/16 → first /29 = 10.0.0.0/29; second = 10.0.0.8/29; ... increment = 8 in last octet; wraps through all 256 values of third octet then second. Verification: 8192 subnets × 8 addresses = 65536 = 2^16 ✓. Use case: /29 is common for point-to-point links or small server subnets needing exactly 6 host addresses.
aspect_key: /16 divided into maximum /29 subnets

question: A security auditor captures a session between an admin workstation and a network switch and finds that all packets contain readable ASCII text including the string "enable secret". What protocol is confirmed to be in use and what TWO changes should be made to the device configuration to prevent this?
choice_a: The session uses SNMP — disable SNMP and enable SNMPv3 to encrypt the community strings
choice_b: The session uses Telnet — the two required changes are: (1) configure SSH (ip ssh version 2; crypto key generate rsa; transport input ssh) to replace Telnet; and (2) ensure the enable secret is configured (not enable password) because enable secret uses MD5/bcrypt hashing rather than reversible encoding
choice_c: The session uses HTTP management — disable HTTP and enable HTTPS on the management interface
choice_d: The session uses FTP for configuration backup — replace FTP with SFTP and restrict access with an ACL
explanation: Telnet identification from capture: port 23 + ASCII payload. "enable secret" visible in capture = Telnet confirmed. Fix 1 — deploy SSH: crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048; ip ssh version 2; on VTY lines: transport input ssh; login local. Fix 2 — enable secret vs enable password: enable password stores reversible type-7 encoded value (easily decoded). enable secret stores hashed value (MD5 or bcrypt) — "enable secret <password>" is the correct command. Additional hardening: aaa new-model; username with privilege; ACL on VTY lines to restrict source IPs. Both fixes address different attack surfaces: SSH prevents capture; enable secret protects the password if config is exposed.
aspect_key: telnet identification and dual remediation ssh plus enable secret

question: The subnet 192.168.100.0/23 is assigned to a department. What is the broadcast address; the valid host range; and how many usable hosts exist?
choice_a: /23 broadcast is 192.168.100.255 and provides 254 usable hosts — same as a /24
choice_b: /23 spans two Class C networks: 192.168.100.0 through 192.168.101.255. Network address: 192.168.100.0. Broadcast: 192.168.101.255 (all host bits set). Valid hosts: 192.168.100.1 through 192.168.101.254. Usable count: 2^9-2 = 510 hosts
choice_c: /23 broadcast is 192.168.101.0 because the odd third octet is the broadcast value
choice_d: /23 provides 1022 usable hosts because it spans four Class C networks
explanation: /23 calculation: mask = 255.255.254.0 (254=11111110). Host bits: 32-23=9. Total: 2^9=512. Usable: 510. Network bits include 7 bits of the third octet. Third octet: 100=01100100; mask octet 254=11111110 → network bit in third octet is the 8th bit (value 1); host bit is the LSB. 192.168.100.x and 192.168.101.x are both in the same /23. Network: 192.168.100.0 (third octet LSB=0). Broadcast: 192.168.101.255 (third octet LSB=1; fourth octet=11111111). /23 is commonly used to double a /24 allocation when a department outgrows 254 hosts.
aspect_key: /23 subnet spanning two class c networks

question: An engineer receives IPv6 address 2001:DB8::1/64 and needs to determine the network prefix and the interface identifier portion. Break down the address into its two components.
choice_a: The /64 prefix is 2001:DB8:: and the interface ID is ::1 — these are the two equal halves
choice_b: The full expanded address is 2001:0DB8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001. The /64 network prefix is the first 64 bits: 2001:0DB8:0000:0000 (first four groups). The interface identifier is the last 64 bits: 0000:0000:0000:0001 (last four groups). The /64 boundary always falls between the 4th and 5th groups in IPv6
choice_c: A /64 prefix means the first 64 hex characters form the network — that would be the first 16 groups
choice_d: The /64 divides the 128-bit address at bit 64; but the groups are 16 bits each so the split falls in the middle of the 4th group rather than between groups
explanation: IPv6 /64 structure: 128 bits total; /64 splits exactly between group 4 and group 5. Expanded: 2001:0DB8:0000:0000 | 0000:0000:0000:0001. Network prefix (/64): 2001:0DB8:0000:0000::/64 (first 4 groups = 64 bits). Interface ID: 0000:0000:0000:0001 (last 4 groups = 64 bits). Why /64 is standard: SLAAC (Stateless Address Autoconfiguration) requires exactly /64 — the interface ID is auto-generated from the MAC address (EUI-64) or randomly. The /64 boundary aligning with group boundaries is by design in IPv6 architecture.
aspect_key: /64 prefix and interface id split

question: A company has public block 198.51.100.0/24. They want to assign a /30 to each of their 60 point-to-point WAN links. Does the /24 have enough space; and what is the maximum number of /30s that fit?
choice_a: A /24 contains 32 /30 subnets — not enough for 60 links
choice_b: A /24 contains 2^(30-24) = 2^6 = 64 /30 subnets; each /30 has 2 usable hosts (2^2-2=2); 64 ≥ 60 so yes the /24 has enough space for all 60 point-to-point links with 4 /30s remaining
choice_c: A /24 contains 128 /30 subnets but /30 subnets cannot be used for point-to-point links because they have only 2 usable hosts
choice_d: A /24 must be broken into /28s first before /30s can be allocated
explanation: /24 to /30 calculation: bits borrowed = 30-24 = 6. Number of /30s: 2^6 = 64. Each /30: 2 host bits → 4 addresses → 2 usable (network + host1 + host2 + broadcast). 64 /30s available; 60 needed → fits with 4 spare. Mask: /30 = 255.255.255.252. Example: 198.51.100.0/30 (hosts .1 and .2); 198.51.100.4/30 (hosts .5 and .6); ... increment = 4. 64 subnets × 4 addresses = 256 = 2^8 ✓. Point-to-point use case: each WAN link needs exactly 2 routable IPs — /30 is the standard choice (wastes only 2 addresses per link vs /29 wasting 6).
aspect_key: /24 into /30 subnets for point-to-point links

question: An organization runs a private network using 10.0.0.0/8 internally. A packet from host 10.5.1.20 destined for 10.5.1.255 is sent on the wire. What type of address is 10.5.1.255 in the context of a /24 subnet; what happens to this packet at layer 2; and which devices process it?
choice_a: 10.5.1.255 is the network address of the 10.5.1.0/24 subnet — routers drop it as invalid
choice_b: If the subnet is 10.5.1.0/24 then 10.5.1.255 is the directed broadcast address — at layer 2 it is sent as a frame with destination MAC FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF; every device on the 10.5.1.0/24 segment receives and processes it at the IP layer; routers do not forward directed broadcasts by default (ip directed-broadcast is disabled by default on Cisco IOS since 12.0)
choice_c: 10.5.1.255 is a multicast address — only devices that have joined the multicast group process it
choice_d: 10.5.1.255 is a valid unicast address — only the device configured with that IP address processes it
explanation: Directed broadcast: highest address in a subnet (all host bits = 1). 10.5.1.255 in a /24 context: host bits = last octet = 11111111 → broadcast. Layer 2 behavior: IP stack resolves broadcast IP → MAC FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF. Ethernet frame floods to all devices on segment. Every host receives the frame; NIC passes to IP stack; IP delivers to application (UDP only typically). Router behavior: by default Cisco IOS does NOT forward directed broadcasts between subnets (prevents Smurf amplification attacks). ip directed-broadcast command enables forwarding (legacy; dangerous). Contrast: 255.255.255.255 = limited broadcast (never routed); directed broadcast = routed within same subnet only.
aspect_key: directed broadcast address and layer 2 behavior

question: Convert the subnet mask 255.255.255.248 to both prefix notation and binary; then calculate how many subnets of this size fit within a 192.168.10.0/24 block.
choice_a: 255.255.255.248 = /28 and provides 16 subnets per /24 with 14 usable hosts
choice_b: 255.255.255.248 in binary is 11111111.11111111.11111111.11111000 — counting the 1-bits gives /29; a /24 contains 2^(29-24)=2^5=32 such subnets; each /29 has 3 host bits providing 2^3-2=6 usable hosts
choice_c: 255.255.255.248 = /30 with 64 subnets per /24 and 2 usable hosts each
choice_d: 255.255.255.248 = /27 with 8 subnets per /24 and 30 usable hosts each
explanation: Binary conversion: 255=11111111 (×3). 248=11111000. Count 1-bits: 8+8+8+5=29 → /29. /29 within /24: bits borrowed=29-24=5. Subnets: 2^5=32. Host bits: 32-29=3. Total per subnet: 2^3=8. Usable: 8-2=6. Subnet increment: 256-248=8 (last octet increments by 8). Example subnets: 192.168.10.0/29; .8/29; .16/29; .24/29; ... .248/29. 32 subnets × 8 addresses = 256 = 2^8 ✓. Key value memorization: 248=11111000 → 5 bits set in last octet (not 4) → /29 not /28. Common trap: counting wrong number of bits in 248.
aspect_key: 255.255.255.248 binary conversion and subnet count

question: A Windows administrator wants to determine which process is listening on port 8080. What command shows the process ID (PID) alongside open ports; and once the PID is found; how is the process name identified?
choice_a: Use netstat -a to see ports; then ping the port to identify the process
choice_b: Use netstat -ano to display all connections with the owning PID; then use tasklist | findstr <PID> or check Task Manager's Details tab to match the PID to the process name
choice_c: Use ipconfig /all which includes a port-to-process mapping in its extended output
choice_d: Use arp -a to display the process binding table which maps PIDs to open ports
explanation: Command sequence: netstat -ano → shows all connections; -o adds PID column. Look for 0.0.0.0:8080 LISTENING → note PID (e.g. 4512). tasklist | findstr 4512 → shows process name (e.g. java.exe). Alternative: Task Manager → Details tab → PID column. PowerShell: Get-NetTCPConnection -LocalPort 8080 | Select-Object -ExpandProperty OwningProcess → PID. Then Get-Process -Id <PID>. Use case: determine if port 8080 is a legitimate app (Tomcat; Jenkins) or malware. Also useful: tasklist /svc /fi "PID eq <n>" shows Windows service name if it's a service process. netstat -b (admin required) shows process name directly in the output.
aspect_key: netstat -ano pid to process identification

question: An organization's two offices use overlapping address space: Office A uses 192.168.1.0/24 and Office B also uses 192.168.1.0/24. They want to connect the offices via VPN. Why does this cause a routing problem and what is the only viable long-term solution?
choice_a: Overlapping subnets cause VPN tunnels to fail because VPN requires unique MAC addresses; renaming the subnets resolves the issue
choice_b: Both offices share the same network address (192.168.1.0/24) — when a host at Office A (e.g. 192.168.1.50) tries to reach a host at Office B (e.g. 192.168.1.80); the router sees the destination as local (same subnet) and never routes it through the VPN; the only long-term solution is to renumber one office to a non-overlapping RFC1918 range
choice_c: Overlapping subnets only cause problems when both offices use dynamic routing — static routes resolve the overlap
choice_d: NAT can permanently solve overlapping subnets without renumbering — configure NAT on both ends to translate identical ranges
explanation: Routing conflict explained: a router makes forwarding decisions using the routing table. If 192.168.1.0/24 is the connected subnet on the local interface; any destination matching 192.168.1.x is considered on-link — the packet is ARP'd locally; never forwarded to VPN. The remote office hosts are unreachable. Workaround (temporary): NAT on VPN endpoints translates one side's addresses (e.g. Office B 192.168.1.x appears as 10.0.99.x to Office A). Limitations of NAT workaround: breaks some protocols; complex to maintain. Best solution: renumber Office B to 192.168.2.0/24 or any non-overlapping RFC1918 range. Prevention: use enterprise-scale 10.0.0.0/8 with unique /24 allocations per site.
aspect_key: overlapping rfc1918 space vpn routing conflict

question: A Telnet session to a router is interrupted mid-command. The administrator reopens a terminal and runs telnet 10.1.1.1. The connection succeeds but the router shows "Password required but none set." What does this mean and how is it resolved?
choice_a: The router has no enable secret configured — add enable secret <password> to fix the Telnet password error
choice_b: The VTY lines have no password configured — Cisco IOS requires a password on VTY lines before allowing Telnet access; the fix is to enter line vty 0 4; set password <password>; set login; on the router; without this any Telnet attempt is rejected with this message regardless of whether a user account exists
choice_c: The router OS has been corrupted — the password database is missing and requires a full IOS reinstall
choice_d: The administrator must first SSH to the router and enable Telnet separately — Telnet cannot be enabled directly via console
explanation: Cisco VTY line requirements for Telnet: line vty 0 4 (or 0 15). password <password> (line password) OR login local (use local usernames). login (required — enables authentication). Without password + login configured: "Password required but none set" error. Fix options: Option 1 (line password): line vty 0 4; password cisco123; login. Option 2 (local auth — better): username admin secret <pw>; line vty 0 4; login local. Option 3 (best — SSH only): transport input ssh; login local. Note: "password required but none set" is a VTY config issue — not related to enable secret or enable password.
aspect_key: vty line password configuration for telnet access

question: Express the IPv4 subnet mask 255.240.0.0 as a prefix length; calculate the number of usable hosts; and identify which classful address class this mask was never the default for.
choice_a: 255.240.0.0 = /12; provides 2^20-2 = 1048574 usable hosts; this mask was never a classful default for Class C networks
choice_b: 255.240.0.0 = /16; this is the default Class B mask providing 65534 hosts
choice_c: 255.240.0.0 = /8; this is the default Class A mask providing 16777214 hosts
choice_d: 255.240.0.0 = /20; provides 4094 usable hosts per subnet
explanation: Binary: 255=11111111; 240=11110000; 0=00000000; 0=00000000. Bit count: 8+4+0+0=12 → /12. Host bits: 32-12=20. Usable: 2^20-2=1048576-2=1048574. This /12 mask was never the default for any classful class: Class A default = /8; Class B default = /16; Class C default = /24. /12 is a CIDR (classless) prefix — it is exactly the mask used for the RFC1918 172.16.0.0/12 private range (172.16.0.0 to 172.31.255.255). The 240 octet (11110000) appears in /12 as the second-octet partial mask — recognizing 240=11110000=4 bits is essential for rapid mask analysis.
aspect_key: 255.240.0.0 binary conversion and host calculation

question: An ISP has Autonomous System number 65001 and peers with AS 65002 and AS 65003. Describe what an Autonomous System is; why AS numbers are used in BGP; and distinguish between private and public AS numbers.
choice_a: An Autonomous System is a single router — each router on the internet has a unique AS number assigned by IANA directly
choice_b: An Autonomous System (AS) is a collection of IP networks under a single administrative domain with a unified routing policy — AS numbers identify each AS uniquely in BGP; BGP uses AS numbers to prevent routing loops (a router rejects routes containing its own AS number in the AS path); private AS numbers (64512–65535) are used for internal BGP sessions or when AS numbers don't need to appear on the public internet; public AS numbers (1–64511 and 131072–4199999999) are assigned by RIRs
choice_c: AS numbers are only used in OSPF — BGP uses IP addresses to identify routing domains
choice_d: Private AS numbers are 1–1023 (reserved for enterprise use) and public AS numbers are 1024 and above
explanation: AS number details: 16-bit range: 1–65535. Public ASNs: 1–64511 (assigned by RIRs; globally unique). Private ASNs: 64512–65535 (like RFC1918 — not routed on public internet). 32-bit ASNs (RFC 4893): extend range to 4294967295; written as plain numbers or X.Y notation. Loop prevention: BGP AS path attribute lists every AS traversed — if a router sees its own AS in the path; it rejects the route. iBGP vs eBGP: iBGP = BGP within an AS (same AS number); eBGP = BGP between different ASes. RIR assignment: organizations needing public ASN apply to ARIN/RIPE/APNIC with justification.
aspect_key: autonomous system numbers public vs private

question: A router running Windows has two interfaces: one with 10.10.10.1/24 connected to the LAN and one with 203.0.113.5/24 connected to the internet. A LAN host at 10.10.10.50 pings 8.8.8.8. Describe every step of the routing process from the host sending the packet to it reaching the internet interface.
choice_a: The host pings 8.8.8.8 directly — no routing decisions are needed because ping uses MAC addresses not IP addresses
choice_b: Host sends ICMP echo to 8.8.8.8. Host checks: is 8.8.8.8 in 10.10.10.0/24? No → forward to default gateway (10.10.10.1). Host ARPs for 10.10.10.1 MAC → sends frame to router. Router receives frame on LAN interface; strips L2 header; reads IP dest 8.8.8.8. Router checks routing table: 8.8.8.8 matches default route (0.0.0.0/0) → exit via internet interface 203.0.113.5. Router decrements TTL; sets new src MAC as internet interface MAC; sends frame toward ISP next hop
choice_c: The host sends the packet to the router which changes the destination IP to its own internet address before forwarding — this is basic routing without NAT
choice_d: The router broadcasts the packet out all interfaces simultaneously and 8.8.8.8 responds to the interface that is closest geographically
explanation: Step-by-step routing: 1. Host compares 8.8.8.8 to local subnet 10.10.10.0/24 → not local. 2. Host sends to default gateway 10.10.10.1 (ARP if needed). 3. L2 frame: src=host MAC; dst=router LAN MAC; L3: src=10.10.10.50; dst=8.8.8.8. 4. Router receives on LAN int; L2 stripped. 5. Routing table lookup: 8.8.8.8 → default route → internet interface. 6. Router may NAT (10.10.10.50 → 203.0.113.5) if PAT configured. 7. New L2 frame: src=router WAN MAC; dst=ISP gateway MAC. 8. Packet forwarded toward internet. TTL decremented at each router hop.
aspect_key: end-to-end routing from lan host to internet

question: A technician connects a laptop to a Cisco switch console port and sees the switch prompt Switch>. After typing enable the switch shows Switch#. Explain the significance of these two prompts; what privilege level each represents; and why the # prompt requires additional credentials.
choice_a: Switch> and Switch# are cosmetic differences only — both provide identical access to all IOS commands
choice_b: Switch> is user EXEC mode (privilege level 1) — only basic non-destructive commands are available (ping; show version; traceroute). Switch# is privileged EXEC mode (privilege level 15) — all commands including show running-config; configure terminal; copy; erase are available. Moving from > to # requires the enable command and the enable secret (or enable password) if configured. Without the # prompt; no configuration changes can be made
choice_c: Switch> means the switch is in ROMMON mode — the IOS has not loaded. Switch# means IOS is loaded and the switch is operational
choice_d: The > prompt means the console password has not been set — the # prompt appears automatically after a password is configured
explanation: IOS privilege model: User EXEC (>) = privilege level 1. Limited read-only commands. Privileged EXEC (#) = privilege level 15. Full command access. Transition: enable command → prompts for enable secret if set → grants # prompt. Configuration hierarchy from #: configure terminal → global config (config)#. interface → interface config (config-if)#. line vty → line config (config-line)#. Security practice: always configure enable secret (not enable password) — enable secret is hashed; enable password is weakly encoded. Without enable secret configured; typing enable grants # without credentials (security risk).
aspect_key: ios user exec vs privileged exec prompts

question: What is the difference between a public IP address and a private IP address in terms of routability; and give a specific scenario where a device with only a private IP address can still access the public internet.
choice_a: Public IPs are routed everywhere; private IPs (RFC1918) cannot be routed on the public internet at all — devices with only private IPs have no internet access under any circumstances
choice_b: Public IP addresses are globally unique and routable across the internet — private IP addresses (10.x.x.x; 172.16-31.x.x; 192.168.x.x) are non-routable on the public internet but devices using them CAN access the internet via NAT (Network Address Translation); a home router performs PAT (Port Address Translation) translating many private IPs to a single public IP — outbound connections work; unsolicited inbound connections require port forwarding
choice_c: Public IPs are used only by servers; private IPs are used only by client devices — the distinction is based on device role not address type
choice_d: Private IPs are more secure than public IPs because they cannot be targeted by internet attackers — only public IPs are exposed
explanation: Public vs private routability: Public IP: globally unique; ISP routes packets to/from it. Private IP: defined in RFC1918; ISP routers DROP packets with RFC1918 source/dest at their borders. NAT mechanism: home user 192.168.1.50 → router NAT → public IP 203.0.113.5 (with ephemeral port). Return traffic: 203.0.113.5:port → router NAT table → translated back to 192.168.1.50. Result: private IP device appears to the internet as the public IP. Limitations: NAT breaks true end-to-end connectivity. Inbound connections require port forwarding or DMZ. VoIP; gaming; P2P may have NAT traversal issues. IPv6 goal: eliminate NAT by giving every device a globally routable address.
aspect_key: nat enables private ip internet access

question: An engineer needs to verify connectivity to a remote host using both ICMP and TCP. Describe the difference in what ping and telnet <host> <port> test; and explain a scenario where ping succeeds but telnet fails.
choice_a: Ping tests TCP connectivity; telnet tests ICMP — they are interchangeable tools for connectivity testing
choice_b: Ping sends ICMP echo requests testing basic IP reachability and round-trip time — it does NOT test whether a specific TCP service is accepting connections; telnet <host> <port> tests whether a specific TCP port is open and the service is listening; a scenario where ping succeeds but telnet fails: the host is reachable (ICMP allowed) but a firewall blocks TCP port 80 or the web service is not running — ping to a web server succeeds; telnet <server> 80 fails with "Connection refused" or timeout
choice_c: Ping tests layer 7 application health; telnet tests only layer 1 physical connectivity
choice_d: Both tools test the same thing — if ping works then telnet to any port on that host will also work
explanation: Diagnostic comparison: Ping = ICMP echo (type 8) → ICMP echo reply (type 0). Tests: IP routing; ICMP policy; basic reachability. Does NOT test: TCP ports; application services; firewalls filtering specific ports. Telnet <host> <port> = TCP SYN to specified port. Tests: TCP three-way handshake; port open; service accepting connections. Scenario ping works / telnet fails: firewall permits ICMP but blocks TCP 443 (HTTPS). Windows firewall blocking specific port. Service crashed (port closed). ACL on router permitting ICMP but denying TCP 22. Diagnostic value: ping eliminates L3 issues; telnet to specific port isolates L4/firewall/application issues.
aspect_key: ping vs telnet port test diagnostic comparison

question: A network engineer must summarize four contiguous /24 networks into a single supernet: 192.168.4.0/24; 192.168.5.0/24; 192.168.6.0/24; 192.168.7.0/24. What is the summary route and prefix?
choice_a: The four networks summarize to 192.168.4.0/21 — four /24s always combine into a /21
choice_b: The four networks summarize to 192.168.4.0/22 — find the common bits in the third octet: 4=00000100; 5=00000101; 6=00000110; 7=00000111; the first 6 bits (000001) are common; the last 2 bits vary; combined with the first 16 bits gives /22 (16+6=22); the summary is 192.168.4.0/22
choice_c: The four networks cannot be summarized because they span two Class C boundaries
choice_d: The summary is 192.168.0.0/22 — route summarization always resets the variable octets to zero
explanation: Summarization method: List third octets: 4; 5; 6; 7. Convert to binary: 4=00000100; 5=00000101; 6=00000110; 7=00000111. Find common prefix bits: 000001xx — first 6 bits match; last 2 vary. Total prefix: 16 (first two octets) + 6 = 22 → /22. Summary network: set varying bits to 0 → 192.168.4.0 (third octet 00000100 with last 2 bits=0). Summary: 192.168.4.0/22. Range check: 192.168.4.0 to 192.168.7.255 — covers all four /24s ✓. Total addresses: 2^10=1024. Pre-requisite: the four networks must be contiguous AND start on a /22 boundary (4 is divisible by 4 ✓).
aspect_key: route summarization of four contiguous /24 networks

question: What command sequence fully prepares a Cisco switch for SSH access from scratch; assuming no prior configuration? List the minimum required steps in order.
choice_a: Enable SSH with the command ssh enable — no other configuration is required
choice_b: The minimum steps are: (1) set hostname (hostname SW1); (2) set domain name (ip domain-name corp.local); (3) generate RSA keys (crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048); (4) create a local user (username admin secret <pw>); (5) configure VTY lines (line vty 0 4; transport input ssh; login local); (6) set SSH version (ip ssh version 2)
choice_c: SSH works by default on all Cisco switches — only the username and password need to be set
choice_d: Only two steps are needed: generate RSA keys and set the enable secret — the VTY lines auto-configure for SSH when keys exist
explanation: SSH prerequisite chain — each step is required: Hostname: RSA key name includes hostname; "Router" key is invalid for production. Domain name: RSA key is named hostname.domainname; required before key generation. RSA key generation: crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048 (minimum 768 for SSHv2; 2048 recommended). Local user: login local on VTY requires at least one username in local database. VTY config: transport input ssh (prevents Telnet fallback); login local (uses local user DB). SSH version: ip ssh version 2 forces SSHv2 only (SSHv1 has known vulnerabilities). Verification: show ip ssh; show ssh; ssh -l admin <router-ip> from another device.
aspect_key: minimum cisco ios ssh configuration steps

question: A host has IP 192.168.50.200/26. Determine the subnet it belongs to; the broadcast address; and the first and last usable host addresses without a calculator.
choice_c: The /26 block containing .200 starts at .196 because 200 ÷ 4 = 50 and 50 × 4 = 200 minus the offset
choice_d: 192.168.50.200/26 belongs to 192.168.50.0/26 because /26 always starts at .0
explanation: /26 analysis: mask = 255.255.255.192. Fourth octet mask = 192 = 11000000. Subnet increment: 256-192=64. Subnet boundaries in fourth octet: 0; 64; 128; 192; (256). 200 falls in range 192–255 → subnet 192.168.50.192/26. Network: 192.168.50.192. Broadcast: 192.168.50.255 (192+64-1 = 255). First usable: 192.168.50.193. Last usable: 192.168.50.254. Total: 64 addresses; 62 usable. Mental shortcut: divide host octet by increment → 200÷64=3.125 → floor=3 → subnet starts at 3×64=192.
aspect_key: /26 subnet membership first last host broadcast

question: A router's ARP table shows that 10.1.1.1 maps to MAC aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff. Ten minutes later a new entry shows 10.1.1.1 maps to MAC 11:22:33:44:55:66. What attack does this pattern indicate and what is the impact on traffic?
choice_a: The new ARP entry means the router was upgraded and received a new MAC address — this is normal ARP table maintenance
choice_b: This pattern indicates ARP poisoning (ARP spoofing) — an attacker sent gratuitous ARP replies falsely claiming that 10.1.1.1 is at a new MAC (11:22:33:44:55:66); if successful; all traffic destined for 10.1.1.1 is now sent to the attacker's machine (the owner of the new MAC) enabling a man-in-the-middle attack; the attacker intercepts and may forward traffic transparently
choice_c: ARP entries always change every 10 minutes due to normal ARP cache expiration — two different MACs for one IP is expected
choice_d: The second MAC address is the IPv6 address of the same host expressed in MAC notation — this is normal dual-stack behavior
explanation: ARP spoofing mechanics: attacker sends unsolicited ARP reply: "10.1.1.1 is at 11:22:33:44:55:66". Victims update ARP cache (no authentication in ARP). Traffic for 10.1.1.1 now goes to attacker. Attacker can: silently forward (MITM — intercept credentials; inject data). Drop traffic (DoS). Impact: all hosts on segment affected if gateway IP is poisoned. Detection: arp -a showing same IP with different MAC over time. Defenses: Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) on managed switches. Static ARP entries for critical hosts. Network monitoring for ARP anomalies. ARP spoofing is the basis for many LAN attacks including SSL stripping and credential harvesting.
aspect_key: arp spoofing detection from arp cache anomaly

question: An engineer uses tracert and observes that hops 4 through 8 all show "* * *" (three asterisks each) but hop 9 responds normally with a low RTT. What does this indicate and is there a connectivity problem?
choice_a: Three asterisks on hops 4–8 means those routers are completely unreachable and the path is broken at hop 4
choice_b: Three asterisks on hops 4–8 but a responding hop 9 means those intermediate routers are reachable (traffic passes through them) but they are configured to not respond to ICMP TTL-expired messages — either by firewall policy or router configuration; there is NO connectivity problem since hop 9 (and presumably the destination) responds; the * * * rows simply mean those routers silently discard the TTL-exceeded ICMP replies
choice_c: Three asterisks means the path has asymmetric routing — replies take a different path than the probes
choice_d: Three asterisks means the hops are wireless segments — tracert cannot display RTT for wireless links
explanation: tracert * * * interpretation: tracert works by sending packets with incrementing TTL. When TTL expires at a router → router SHOULD send ICMP Time Exceeded back. If router silently drops the TTL-expired packet (ICMP rate-limiting; ACL blocking ICMP; or deliberate admin policy) → * * * displayed. But transit traffic is NOT affected — the router still forwards packets; it just doesn't reply to TTL-expired messages. Key indicator: if a hop BEYOND the * * * rows responds → path is intact; those intermediate hops are just ICMP-silent. True break: * * * continuing to the destination with no final response = actual connectivity problem. ISPs frequently configure core routers to suppress ICMP TTL-exceeded.
aspect_key: tracert asterisks icmp silent routers vs real breaks

question: What is the difference between a DNS A record and a DNS AAAA record; and why would an administrator add both for the same hostname?
choice_a: A records store IPv6 addresses; AAAA records store IPv4 addresses — they are named for the number of address octets
choice_b: An A record maps a hostname to an IPv4 address (32-bit); an AAAA record maps a hostname to an IPv6 address (128-bit); adding both for the same hostname enables dual-stack operation — a client that supports IPv6 receives the AAAA record and connects via IPv6; a client that only supports IPv4 falls back to the A record; this allows a server to be reachable over both protocols simultaneously
choice_c: A records and AAAA records are interchangeable — the extra A in AAAA simply marks the record as authoritative
choice_d: AAAA records are used only for internal hostnames; A records are used only for internet-facing hostnames
explanation: DNS record types for host resolution: A record: hostname → 32-bit IPv4 address. e.g. www.example.com → 93.184.216.34. AAAA record: hostname → 128-bit IPv6 address. e.g. www.example.com → 2606:2800:220:1:248:1893:25c8:1946. AAAA = "quad-A" — named because IPv6 is 4× the size of IPv4 (128 vs 32 bits). Dual-stack DNS: both records coexist for the same hostname. Client behavior: IPv6-capable OS queries for AAAA first (RFC 6724 default); falls back to A if no AAAA or IPv6 path fails. Admin use: add AAAA when deploying IPv6 for a service — existing IPv4 clients unaffected; IPv6 clients use faster/more direct path.
aspect_key: dns a record vs aaaa record dual stack

question: A host is configured with IP 172.31.255.200/12. Using bitwise AND; identify the network address and explain whether this address is within the RFC1918 172.16.0.0/12 private range.
choice_a: 172.31.255.200/12 is in a different subnet than the RFC1918 range because 172.31 differs from 172.16
choice_c: 172.31.255.200/12 network address is 172.31.0.0 because the /12 only masks the first octet and part of the second octet gives 172.31
choice_d: The /12 mask covers the entire second octet so the network is 172.0.0.0
explanation: Bitwise AND: 172 AND 255 = 172. 31 AND 240: 31=00011111; 240=11110000; AND=00010000=16. 255 AND 0 = 0. 200 AND 0 = 0. Network: 172.16.0.0/12. RFC1918 /12 range: 172.16.0.0 (network) to 172.31.255.255 (broadcast). 172.31.255.200: is 31 within 16-31? Yes (16 ≤ 31 ≤ 31). Is 255.200 valid host? Yes (not all zeros or all ones relative to /12 network bits). Conclusion: 172.31.255.200 IS within RFC1918 172.16.0.0/12. Key insight: the /12 mask groups the second octet in blocks of 16 (256-240=16); 172.16 through 172.31 are all in the same /12 network.
aspect_key: bitwise and to verify rfc1918 172.16/12 membership

question: An administrator connects to a switch via SSH using key-based authentication and notices that the connection succeeds without entering a password. A colleague claims this is a security vulnerability. Is the colleague correct and why?
choice_a: The colleague is correct — SSH key-based auth is less secure than passwords because the key file can be stolen more easily than a memorized password
choice_b: The colleague is incorrect — SSH key-based authentication is MORE secure than password authentication; the private key on the client authenticates the user cryptographically; the server only stores the public key; even if the server is compromised the private key is never transmitted; additionally key-based auth is immune to brute-force and phishing attacks that target passwords
choice_c: SSH key-based auth is a vulnerability because it allows anyone on the same machine to SSH in — the key is shared among all users on the system
choice_d: The colleague is correct — passwordless access means the connection has no authentication; it relies only on network-level security
explanation: SSH key-based authentication mechanism: Key pair generation: ssh-keygen creates private key (stays on client) + public key (deployed to server ~/.ssh/authorized_keys). Authentication: client proves possession of private key by signing a challenge. Server verifies signature using stored public key. No password transmitted. Security advantages over passwords: Immune to brute-force (no password to guess). Immune to phishing (no password to steal via fake login). Private key never leaves the client. Can add passphrase to private key for additional protection. Potential risks: unprotected private key file (chmod 600 required on Linux). Key compromise if laptop stolen (mitigated by passphrase). Revocation: remove public key from authorized_keys — no need to change passwords on all servers.
aspect_key: ssh key based auth security advantage over passwords

question: An IPv6 address is displayed as ::1. Expand this fully to 128 bits and identify what type of address it is.
choice_a: ::1 expands to 0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001:0000 and is a multicast address
choice_b: ::1 expands to 0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001 — this is the IPv6 loopback address; equivalent to 127.0.0.1 in IPv4; it refers to the local host and is used to test the local IPv6 stack without sending traffic on the network
choice_c: ::1 expands to FFFF:FFFF:FFFF:FFFF:FFFF:FFFF:FFFF:0001 and is the IPv6 broadcast address
choice_d: ::1 expands to 0001:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000 because :: always fills from the left
explanation: ::1 expansion: :: represents one or more groups of 0000. Total groups needed = 8. Groups accounted for after :: collapse: 1 (the final :1 = 0001). Groups represented by ::: 8-1 = 7. Expanded: 0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001. IPv6 loopback: ::1 = 0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001. Defined in RFC 4291. Equivalent to IPv4 127.0.0.1. Only one host per system — the local interface. No traffic sent on wire. Use: ping6 ::1 (Linux) or ping ::1 (Windows) tests local IPv6 stack. Distinction: IPv6 has no loopback /8 range; exactly one loopback address (::1) exists. IPv6 also has no broadcast — multicast replaces it.
aspect_key: ipv6 loopback address expansion and identification

question: A company is planning its IP addressing scheme for a new building with 8 departments averaging 25 hosts each. They want to use a single classful Class C address and subnet it. What is the minimum prefix length that creates enough subnets while supporting 25 hosts each; and does a Class C have enough space?
choice_a: Use /27 — this gives 8 subnets with 30 usable hosts each; 8 × 32 = 256 addresses = exactly one Class C /24; sufficient
choice_b: Use /25 — this gives only 2 subnets which is insufficient for 8 departments
choice_c: Use /28 — this gives 16 subnets with 14 hosts each; 14 < 25 so this is insufficient for the host requirement
choice_d: Use /26 — this gives 4 subnets; not enough for 8 departments
explanation: /27 analysis for 8 departments × 25 hosts: Subnet requirement: need ≥ 8 subnets. /27 borrows 3 bits from a /24 → 2^3=8 subnets ✓. Host requirement: need ≥ 25 usable hosts. /27 host bits=5 → 2^5-2=30 usable ✓ (30 ≥ 25). Mask: 255.255.255.224. Space check: 8 subnets × 32 addresses = 256 = exactly one /24 ✓. Example allocation: 192.168.1.0/27; .32/27; .64/27; .96/27; .128/27; .160/27; .192/27; .224/27. Why not /26: gives 4 subnets (not enough for 8). Why not /28: gives 16 subnets (enough) but only 14 hosts (insufficient for 25). /27 is the only prefix that satisfies both constraints simultaneously within a /24.
aspect_key: class c subnetting for 8 departments 25 hosts each

question: The Windows command ipconfig /displaydns shows a cached entry for "badsite.com" pointing to 1.2.3.4 but the administrator knows the real IP should be 5.6.7.8. The hosts file has no entry for badsite.com. What are the two most likely explanations and what commands address each?
choice_a: The DNS cache is read-only — the only fix is to reinstall Windows to clear DNS entries
choice_b: Explanation 1: The DNS server returned 1.2.3.4 (possibly misconfigured or the record has not propagated yet) — fix with ipconfig /flushdns to clear the cache; the next lookup queries the DNS server fresh. Explanation 2: DNS poisoning — a malicious DNS response has polluted the resolver cache — fix with ipconfig /flushdns; then investigate the DNS server and network for signs of attack; also check if DNSSEC is configured
choice_c: DNS cache entries reflect the hosts file — edit C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts to add badsite.com 5.6.7.8 and the cache will auto-update
choice_d: The ipconfig /displaydns command only shows estimated future DNS responses — the actual cache does not store IP addresses
explanation: DNS cache troubleshooting: ipconfig /displaydns → shows all cached entries (name; type; TTL; data). Entry showing wrong IP sources: Legitimate: DNS propagation delay (record changed; old TTL not expired yet). Stale entry: TTL-based caching of an old value. DNS cache poisoning: attacker injected false record. Fix: ipconfig /flushdns → clears entire resolver cache → next query goes to DNS server. Verify: after flush; nslookup badsite.com → confirms what server returns. If server returns wrong IP: DNS server misconfigured or server itself is poisoned. Security hardening: DNSSEC validates DNS responses cryptographically. Monitor DNS server logs for unusual query patterns.
aspect_key: dns cache wrong entry diagnosis and flush

question: What are the three RFC1918 private address ranges; what is the total number of addresses in each; and rank them from largest to smallest?
choice_a: The three RFC1918 ranges are 10.0.0.0/8 (16777216 addresses); 172.16.0.0/12 (1048576 addresses); 192.168.0.0/16 (65536 addresses) — ranked largest to smallest: 10/8 > 172.16/12 > 192.168/16
choice_b: The three ranges are 10.0.0.0/8; 172.16.0.0/16; and 192.168.0.0/24 — all provide 256 addresses per network
choice_c: The three ranges are 10.0.0.0/8; 172.0.0.0/12; and 192.168.0.0/16 — 172.0.0.0 is the correct starting address for the middle range
choice_d: RFC1918 defines only two ranges — 10.0.0.0/8 and 192.168.0.0/16; the 172.16.0.0 range is a CIDR range not part of RFC1918
explanation: RFC1918 ranges (RFC 1918 — Address Allocation for Private Internets): 10.0.0.0/8: 2^24 = 16777216 addresses (10.0.0.0–10.255.255.255). 172.16.0.0/12: 2^20 = 1048576 addresses (172.16.0.0–172.31.255.255). 192.168.0.0/16: 2^16 = 65536 addresses (192.168.0.0–192.168.255.255). Ranked by size: 10/8 (16M) > 172.16/12 (1M) > 192.168/16 (65K). All three: non-routable on public internet; ISP routers drop RFC1918 source/dest. Use: 10/8 = large enterprise; 172.16/12 = medium/data center; 192.168/16 = home/SOHO. Common trap: 172.16.0.0/12 extends to 172.31.x.x (not 172.16.x.x only).
aspect_key: all three rfc1918 ranges addresses and size ranking

question: A CIDR block 10.0.0.0/8 is to be divided into equal /10 subnets. How many /10 subnets are produced; what is the mask for each; and what is the address range of the second /10 subnet?
choice_a: A /8 divided into /10 subnets produces 4 subnets; mask 255.192.0.0; second subnet is 10.64.0.0/10 (10.64.0.0 to 10.127.255.255)
choice_b: A /8 divided into /10 produces 2 subnets — the /10 is only 2 bits wider than /8 so only 2 subnets fit
choice_c: A /8 divided into /10 produces 8 subnets each covering 4 million addresses
choice_d: A /8 divided into /10 produces 16 subnets with mask 255.255.0.0
explanation: /8 to /10 calculation: bits borrowed = 10-8 = 2. Subnets: 2^2 = 4. Each /10 host bits: 32-10=22 → 2^22=4194304 addresses. Mask: 255.192.0.0 (second octet: 11000000=192). Increment: 256-192=64 in second octet. Subnet 1: 10.0.0.0/10 (10.0.0.0–10.63.255.255). Subnet 2: 10.64.0.0/10 (10.64.0.0–10.127.255.255). Subnet 3: 10.128.0.0/10 (10.128.0.0–10.191.255.255). Subnet 4: 10.192.0.0/10 (10.192.0.0–10.255.255.255). Verification: 4 × 4194304 = 16777216 = 2^24 ✓ (total /8 addresses). Real-world use: large ISPs and enterprises divide /8 blocks into /10s or /12s for regional allocation.
aspect_key: dividing a /8 into /10 subnets count and ranges
